intro: Python focuses on fast prototyping and human readability through extensive abstractions, whereas C++ delivers high-performance system execution paired with zero-cost object-oriented abstractions. Porting applications to C++ enables granular control over hardware resources, predictable memory profiles, and optimized compute-heavy throughput. This migration demands shifting from dynamic duck typing to strict compile-time types, generic templates, and explicit resource lifetimes.

diffs.from: Strongly and dynamically checked. Dynamic object resolution allows generic function calling based on method availability.
diffs.to: Strongly and statically checked. Supports complex compile-time types, templates, concepts, and explicit polymorphism via virtual method tables.

diffs.from: Clean indentation layout. No statement terminators, featuring concise built-in declarations like native list or dict literals.
diffs.to: Verbose block-scoped syntax utilizing curly braces, mandatory semicolons, scope resolution operators (::), and explicit access specifiers.

diffs.from: Managed implicitly by an engine tracking reference markers, supplemented by a cycle-detecting garbage collector.
diffs.to: Deterministic scope management using Resource Acquisition Is Initialization (RAII), supported by smart pointers (std::unique_ptr, std::shared_ptr).

diffs.from: Managed via an event-driven asyncio framework using native keyword extensions like async and await.
diffs.to: Implemented via low-level primitives like std::future, std::promise, threads, and modern language coroutines added in C++20.

diffs.from: Comprehensive array of tools for varied tasks ranging from internet operations to data formatting utilities.
diffs.to: Standard Template Library (STL) emphasizing highly efficient algorithms, mathematical operations, streams, and collection structures.

examples.title: Defining and using a key-value map
examples.description: Compares native Python dictionaries to the type-strict std::unordered_map structure.

examples.title: Creating a base class and subclass hierarchy
examples.description: Shows the transformation from runtime dynamic overrides to compile-time virtual method declarations.

tips.title: Leverage RAII and Smart Pointers
tips.body: Avoid raw pointers and explicit delete statements. Use std::unique_ptr and std::shared_ptr to automate resource allocation tracking and cleanup.

tips.title: Pass Large Collections by Reference
tips.body: Python always passes objects by reference reference. In C++, pass objects via constant references (e.g., const std::string& obj) to avoid expensive copy operations.

tips.title: Utilize STL Container Ecosystem
tips.body: Replace Python lists with std::vector, and use std::unordered_map for constant-time hash tables instead of crafting custom storage routines.

tips.title: Embrace Auto Type Deduction
tips.body: Use the 'auto' keyword in C++ to reduce type declaration verbosity, especially when initializing complex iterators or template types.

faqs.question: What is the C++ equivalent of Python's dynamically typed variable?
faqs.answer: You can use `std::variant` (for a specific set of safe types) or `std::any` (for any type) introduced in modern C++17 to mirror dynamic type flexibility safely.

faqs.question: Why is my compiled C++ application executing so much faster than Python?
faqs.answer: C++ compiles directly to native machine assembly instructions and avoids runtime bytecode interpretation, dynamic type checking lookup steps, and garbage collection overhead.

faqs.question: How do I handle string formatting efficiently like Python f-strings?
faqs.answer: In C++20 and later, you can use the `<format>` library via `std::format("Hello {}", name)` to achieve syntax parity with Python f-strings.
